examples: I'm not going to give up that easily.

look for.monolingual: To try to find something or someone.
examples: What are you looking for?

come on.monolingual: Used to encourage or urge someone; also to express disbelief.
examples: Come on, we're going to be late!

turn out.monolingual: To happen in a particular way; to prove to be.
examples: It turned out he was right all along.

figure out.monolingual: To understand or solve something after thinking about it.
examples: I can't figure out how to open this.

pick up.monolingual: To lift; to collect someone; to learn casually.
examples: Can you pick me up at the airport?

get out.monolingual: To leave a place; to escape.
examples: Get out of here before they see you!
What's going on here?
When are you coming back?

show up.monolingual: To arrive or appear, often unexpectedly.
examples: He didn't show up to the meeting.
She'll find out the truth eventually.

hang out.monolingual: To spend time relaxing or socializing.
examples: We used to hang out every weekend.

work out.monolingual: To exercise; to succeed or be resolved.
examples: Everything will work out in the end.

set up.monolingual: To arrange or organize; to establish.
examples: Who set up this whole thing?

end up.monolingual: To finally be in a situation, often unplanned.
examples: We ended up staying until midnight.
I need to get back home before dark.
Hold on, let me check.
Would you just shut up for a second?

wake up.monolingual: To stop sleeping; to become alert.
examples: Wake up! You're going to miss the bus.

move on.monolingual: To progress; to stop dwelling on something.
examples: It's time to move on with your life.

take off.monolingual: To remove (clothing); to depart (plane); to leave quickly.
examples: I need to take off, see you later.

bring up.monolingual: To mention a topic; to raise a child.
examples: Don't bring that up again, please.
They broke up last month.

make up.monolingual: To invent a story; to reconcile after an argument.
examples: Did you guys make up after the fight?
I feel like I'm going to throw up.
She passed out from the heat.
I just wanted to point out one thing.

freak out.monolingual: To become extremely upset or excited.
examples: Don't freak out, but I lost the keys.

put on.monolingual: To dress in; to place on a surface.
examples: Put on a jacket, it's cold outside.

get off.monolingual: To leave a vehicle; to stop touching.
examples: Get off at the next stop.

run out.monolingual: To exhaust a supply; to have none left.
examples: We're running out of time.

go back.monolingual: To return to a previous place or state.
examples: I don't want to go back there.
Keep up the good work!

blow up.monolingual: To explode; to become very angry.
examples: The whole thing blew up in his face.

take out.monolingual: To remove; to bring food from a restaurant.
examples: Let's take out some pizza tonight.

check out.monolingual: To examine; to leave a hotel; to look at.
examples: Check out this view!

grow up.monolingual: To mature; to become an adult.
examples: When I grew up, everything changed.

calm down.monolingual: To become less agitated or emotional.
examples: Calm down, it's going to be okay.

break down.monolingual: To stop functioning; to cry uncontrollably.
examples: The car broke down on the highway.

stand up.monolingual: To rise to a standing position.
examples: Stand up when the judge enters.
Please, sit down and relax.
She finally gave in and said yes.

look out.monolingual: To be careful; a warning of danger.
examples: Look out! There's a car coming!

go ahead.monolingual: To proceed; permission to do something.
examples: Go ahead, I'm listening.

back off.monolingual: To retreat; to stop pressuring someone.
examples: Back off, this is none of your business.
Come in, the door's open.
You should sign up for that class.

drop off.monolingual: To leave someone/something at a place; to decrease.
examples: I'll drop you off at the station.
She cut me off mid-sentence.

pull off.monolingual: To succeed in doing something difficult.
examples: I can't believe you pulled that off!

let go.monolingual: To release one's hold; to stop clinging.
examples: You have to let go of the past.
I don't know how you put up with him.
Just go away and leave me alone.
Look it up online if you don't believe me.
Turn around slowly and don't make any sudden moves.

get in.monolingual: To enter a vehicle or place.
examples: Get in the car, we're leaving.
We need to get away from here.

bring back.monolingual: To return something; to evoke memories.
examples: This song brings back memories.

lock up.monolingual: To imprison; to secure a building.
examples: Lock up when you leave, okay?
One punch and he was knocked out cold.
I really screwed up this time.

mess up.monolingual: To make an error; to ruin.
examples: Don't mess this up for me.
Who's going to take over when the boss retires?

hook up.monolingual: To connect; to have a casual romantic encounter (informal).
examples: Can you hook up the speakers for me?
Let's wrap this up, it's getting late.

fill in.monolingual: To complete a form; to inform someone.
examples: Fill me in on what happened.

cheer up.monolingual: To become happier; to make someone happier.
examples: Cheer up, things will get better.

cool down.monolingual: To become less hot; to calm down.
examples: Let's cool down before we say something we regret.
We should head out before traffic gets bad.
The painkillers should kick in any minute now.
I trusted you and you let me down.

open up.monolingual: To become more communicative; to unlock.
examples: He finally opened up about his feelings.
The troops are going to pull out next month.

run into.monolingual: To meet unexpectedly; to collide with.
examples: I ran into your mom at the grocery store.
We need to sort this out before Monday.

speak up.monolingual: To talk louder; to voice one's opinion.
examples: If you have something to say, speak up.

stand out.monolingual: To be noticeable; to be exceptional.
examples: She really stands out in a crowd.

step up.monolingual: To take action; to increase effort.
examples: Someone needs to step up and take charge.

stick around.monolingual: To stay; to remain in a place.
examples: Stick around, the show's about to start.

take in.monolingual: To absorb information; to shelter someone.
examples: That's a lot to take in all at once.

turn down.monolingual: To reject; to lower the volume.
examples: I can't believe she turned him down.

walk away.monolingual: To leave a situation; to abandon.
examples: Sometimes the best thing to do is walk away.
I'll have one as well, please.

by the way.monolingual: Incidentally; used to introduce a new topic.
examples: By the way, have you seen my keys?
Of course I'll help you.
I'm kind of tired right now.

a lot.monolingual: A large amount or to a great degree.
examples: She talks a lot when she's nervous.
In fact, I was just about to call you.

at least.monolingual: Not less than; at the minimum.
examples: At least tell me why you're leaving.
Wait, are you for real right now?
No way! I can't believe it!
I need you to listen to me right now.
So far, everything is going according to plan.

at all.monolingual: In any way; to any extent (used with negatives).
examples: I don't mind at all.
It's like, you know, complicated.
I mean, it's not like we had a choice.
Could you turn it down a little?
I sort of expected this to happen.
Did you do that on purpose?
At first I didn't understand, but then it clicked.
This time, I'm leaving for good.
Who's in charge around here?
Come here at once!
That's for sure the best one yet.
Did I just say that out loud?

in common.monolingual: Shared between two or more people.
examples: We have a lot more in common than you think.
I know this speech by heart.
That building is on fire!

in trouble.monolingual: In a difficult or dangerous situation.
examples: You're in big trouble, young man.
The train arrived on time for once.
They gave it to us for free!
At last, we can go home.
I think I'm in love with her.
It's not a big deal, don't worry.

what the hell.monolingual: Expression of surprise, anger or confusion.
examples: What the hell are you doing here?

no kidding.monolingual: Really; I'm serious / expression of agreement.
examples: No kidding, that was the best meal I've ever had.
Oh, my bad. I thought you said Tuesday.
Fair enough, I see your point.
Trust me, you don't want to go there.

never mind.monolingual: Forget about it; it doesn't matter.
examples: Never mind, I'll do it myself.

so what.monolingual: Why does it matter; expressing indifference.
examples: So what if I'm late? It doesn't matter.
Take care of yourself out there.
Good luck on your exam tomorrow.
Watch out for that step!
Well done, you passed with flying colors!
Just a heads up, the boss is in a bad mood.
Way to go, champ!

no offense.monolingual: I don't mean to insult you.
examples: No offense, but that's a terrible idea.

give me a break.translation: no me jodas, dame un respiro
give me a break.bilingual: (idiom) no me jodas, dame un respiro
examples: Give me a break, that's ridiculous.

here we go.monolingual: Indicates something is about to start.
examples: Alright, here we go again.
Don't worry, I got it from here.
Thank you so much! — My pleasure.
Not bad for a first attempt!

all along.translation: todo el tiempo, desde el principio
all along.bilingual: (idiom) todo el tiempo, desde el principio
all along.monolingual: The entire time; from the beginning.
examples: She knew the truth all along.

after all.monolingual: Despite what was said or expected.
examples: Maybe it wasn't such a bad idea after all.
Once again, I find myself apologizing.
Ever since that day, nothing was the same.
In the end, it all worked out.
It's all over now, you can relax.
I'll be there right away.
She should be home by now.

as usual.monolingual: In the normal or expected way.
examples: He was late, as usual.
The car broke down, and on top of that, it started raining.

at the end of the day.token: at the end of the day
examples: At the end of the day, family is what matters.
To be honest, I don't really care.

the thing is.translation: el asunto es, la cosa es
the thing is.bilingual: (idiom) el asunto es, la cosa es
the thing is.monolingual: Used to explain or introduce a problem.
examples: The thing is, I already made plans.
What matters is that you're safe.
Long story short, we missed the flight.

for what it is worth.monolingual: My opinion may not be valuable, but...
examples: For what it's worth, I think you made the right call.

later on.monolingual: At some time in the future; afterwards.
examples: Later on, we can discuss the details.
